Welcome back to The Best & The Brightest. I\xe2\x80\x99m Leigh Ann Caldwell, a bit bleary-eyed along with the rest of the Washington media world (and members of Congress) after last night\xe2\x80\x99s stemwinder of a non-State of the Union speech. Before Trump took the mic, I joined NBC News\xe2\x80\x99s Tom Llamas, who was just named as Lester Holt\xe2\x80\x99s successor on Nightly News, and Hallie Jackson on set for their special coverage.

Mar 05, 2025

Leigh Ann Caldwell

Welcome back to The Best & The Brightest. I\xe2\x80\x99m Leigh Ann Caldwell , a bit bleary-eyed along with the rest of the Washington media world (and members of Congress) after last night\xe2\x80\x99s stemwinder of a non\xe2\x80\x93State of the Union speech. Before Trump took the mic, I joined NBC News\xe2\x80\x99s Tom Llamas , who was just named as Lester Holt \xe2\x80\x99s successor on Nightly News , and Hallie Jackson on set for their special coverage. I\xe2\x80\x99m going to spare you the highlights and hot takes\xe2\x80\x94I\xe2\x80\x99m sure you\xe2\x80\x99ve had enough of those already\xe2\x80\x94except to say that whatever you think of Trump , he is a master emcee.

In tonight\xe2\x80\x99s issue, I explore another type of performative politics\xe2\x80\x94Republicans\xe2\x80\x99 contradictory relationship with the president. Privately, of course, many lawmakers are uneasy about the president\xe2\x80\x99s trade wars with Canada and Mexico and China, and at least somewhat concerned about the depth of Elon Musk \xe2\x80\x99s cuts to government functions and services. Publicly, however, most are reciting the mantra that these are all just temporary dislocations on the eventual path to economic nirvana. Along the way, Republicans are also ceding their own powers to a president they can only hope is successful.

Still, there\xe2\x80\x99s one Republican who has been remarkably consistent in his critique that Congress has ceded far too much authority to the executive: Rand Paul . Our conversation about Washington\xe2\x80\x99s crumbling division of power, below the fold.

A MESSAGE FROM OUR SPONSOR

Medicaid funding cuts pose a threat to children, seniors, veterans, and people with disabilities.

But first\xe2\x80\xa6

  • A funding fight prisoner swap : The White House is executing a full-court press to get Republicans behind a funding extension through the rest of the fiscal year ending September 30. Trump hosted the most conservative House members\xe2\x80\x94the likeliest potential holdouts\xe2\x80\x94at the White House today to get them in line. Previously, Office of Management and Budget Director Russell Vought and his likely deputy, former Rep. Dan Bishop , had made their own attempt Monday night\xe2\x80\x94they met with the fiscally hardline Freedom Caucus, and told them that they needed to keep the government\xe2\x80\x99s lights on if they wanted Trump and Musk to keep gutting their least favorite programs. If some Republicans stuck to their \xe2\x80\x9cno\xe2\x80\x9d votes on the funding bill, Vought added, leadership would be forced to exchange concessions for Democratic votes, according to a person familiar.

    Meanwhile, Democrats aren\xe2\x80\x99t keen on a bill that extends funding through the rest of the year, allowing Trump more power to direct spending however he likes. They want a shorter -term funding bill that buys time for writing individual spending bills and inserts more guardrails for Congress to direct how money should be spent. And Democratic and Republican appropriators are still working on this possible Plan B in case Trump\xe2\x80\x99s rest-of-the-year funding bill can\xe2\x80\x99t pass. Confused yet? Either way, if nothing passes, government funding runs out a week from Friday.
  • Tactically sound : As I said, I won\xe2\x80\x99t rehash Trump\xe2\x80\x99s speech, but I can talk about how Democrats reacted to it. Those who attended\xe2\x80\x94many of them didn\xe2\x80\x99t\xe2\x80\x94color-coded their threads in Barbie -affirming pink and held up little auction paddles, or mini white boards, with not-so-subliminal messages written on them (\xe2\x80\x9cLies,\xe2\x80\x9d \xe2\x80\x9cMusk steals,\xe2\x80\x9d \xe2\x80\x9cFalse,\xe2\x80\x9d \xe2\x80\x9cSave Medicaid,\xe2\x80\x9d and the like). They\xe2\x80\x99ve taken some flak for the tactics, organized by the Congressional Progressive Caucus, which critics described as amateurish, petty, and ineffective.

    But participating Democrats thought that doing something was better than doing nothing as Trump held court with millions tuning in. Plus, one progressive Democratic strategist argued that the substance of their message is popular, and one that all Democrats are behind. \xe2\x80\x9cThe right question isn\xe2\x80\x99t, \xe2\x80\x98What do we think of any particular tactic?\xe2\x80\x99\xe2\x80\x9d the strategist said. \xe2\x80\x9cIt\xe2\x80\x99s \xe2\x80\x98Are we using every single tactic we have to raise the salience of Medicaid, vets, and Musk?\xe2\x80\x99 which are great issues for us.\xe2\x80\x9d

Now, a quick update on another D.C. institution, MSNBC, from my partner Dylan Byers \xe2\x80\xa6

Dylan Byers
  • Maddow as Hell, cont\xe2\x80\x99d : Rachel Maddow \xe2\x80\x99s recent crusade against MSNBC\xe2\x80\x99s front office appears to have been even more misguided than I\xe2\x80\x99d initially appreciated. As you\xe2\x80\x99ll recall, the primetime star put her new boss, Rebecca Kutler , on blast last week for canceling Joy Reid \xe2\x80\x99s show and others hosted by people of color\xe2\x80\x94without noting that Reid was being replaced by three hosts of color, and that Kutler was also adding more diversity on the weekends. Maddow also took management to task on live television over the \xe2\x80\x9cindefensible\xe2\x80\x9d decision to fire production staff and ask them to reapply for new jobs\xe2\x80\x94which, as I noted, showed Maddow\xe2\x80\x99s lack of appreciation for both the shrinking media landscape as well as the effect her own $25 million annual salary had on those economics.

    In Maddow\xe2\x80\x99s telling, the firing-and-rehiring practice was \xe2\x80\x9cnot the right way to treat people, and it\xe2\x80\x99s inefficient, and it\xe2\x80\x99s unnecessary.\xe2\x80\x9d But, as I\xe2\x80\x99ve since learned, the reason MSNBC conducts matters this way is due to the demands of the MSNBC union that Maddow herself has long championed. Historically, MSNBC would reassign production staff to other shows in the event of a cancellation. But when the union reached its first collective bargaining agreement, in December 2023, it demanded equal treatment for all employees, which made it harder for the network to reassign certain staffers while laying off others.

    In January 2024, MSNBC made programming changes that affected roughly 75 employees, all of whom were laid off and then invited to reapply for positions with the new shows. The vast majority of these staffers were rehired, while around 10 or so others either took jobs elsewhere or simply weren\xe2\x80\x99t rehired. The same thing is likely to happen this time around. As I noted last week, roughly 125 employees were laid off, and about 110 new positions are on offer. None of this is ideal, of course, but it\xe2\x80\x99s pretty decent by the current standards of the industry.

Now, for the main event\xe2\x80\xa6

Rand Takes His Stand

The libertarian Kentucky senator Rand Paul is one of few Republicans to openly oppose Trump on tariffs and separation of powers. Is anyone listening?

Leigh Ann Caldwell

While the rest of the party had dutifully marched behind the president in tariff-imposing, government-cutting solidarity, Rand Paul has been one of the few Republicans in the House or Senate to publicly criticize Trump . Paul, of course, is a contrarian by nature. He\xe2\x80\x99s also a consistent free-market libertarian who insists that other members of the G.O.P. privately agree with him and \xe2\x80\x9cwhisper under their breath [that] free trade is good.\xe2\x80\x9d

Yesterday, hours after Trump imposed new 25 percent tariffs on Canada and Mexico and additional 10 percent levies on China, I got on the phone with Paul to discuss the policy and his views. At the time, his colleagues in the party were mostly keeping their nerves to themselves, and crossing their fingers and toes that tariffs could somehow benefit the economy in the long run, even as Trump himself admitted there will be some dislocation. North Dakota Senator John Hoeven told me he spoke with Secretary of Agriculture Brooke Rollins about reviving a program used to bail out tariff-afflicted farmers during Trump\xe2\x80\x99s first term, to the tune of $28 billion. \xe2\x80\x9cWe have to hope\xe2\x80\x9d this is the right strategy, Hoeven said.

A MESSAGE FROM OUR SPONSOR

Medicaid funding cuts pose a threat to children, seniors, veterans, and people with disabilities.

Paul\xe2\x80\x99s not indulging such hopes: He thinks tariffs make for bad economics. He also argued that these latest tolls are another attempt by the executive branch to steal power that the Constitution assigned to Congress. (Notably, however, the first law that Congress sent George Washington in 1789 was the Tariff Act, which attempted to raise revenue by placing a 5 percent tax on all imports.) A strict constitutionalist\xe2\x80\x94and an annoyingly punctilious one, to many of his colleagues\xe2\x80\x94Paul doesn\xe2\x80\x99t think this president should get a free pass on separation of powers from Republicans just because he represents their party.

Most of his colleagues, of course, are gladly handing over their authority to a president they\xe2\x80\x99re afraid to cross. While Democrats insist that Elon Musk \xe2\x80\x99s DOGE cuts encroach on Congress\xe2\x80\x99s power of the purse, Speaker Mike Johnson has said that they\xe2\x80\x99re actually the ones committing a \xe2\x80\x9cgross separation of powers violation\xe2\x80\x9d by demanding to protect congressional appropriations. Senator Tim Scott declared on the floor this week that \xe2\x80\x9cnow is not the time to tie the hands of President Trump.\xe2\x80\x9d Anyway, Paul is an outlier, who has consistently diverged from his party\xe2\x80\x99s expansive view of executive power. (He has also long fought to wrest back Congress\xe2\x80\x99s power to declare war, which presidents have pretty much consistently ignored since the last time Congress exercised it in World War II.)

Paul suggested the president probably has the authority to pause government funding, but that Congress has to pass the cuts to make them legal and permanent. As someone who wants the smallest government possible, Paul supports the cuts themselves. And he asked Vice President J.D. Vance during a Senate Republican lunch a few weeks ago to send Congress a package listing all the administration\xe2\x80\x99s cuts\xe2\x80\x94a.k.a., a rescissions package\xe2\x80\x94so that Congress could codify them into law and ensure they aren\xe2\x80\x99t refunded. Rand made the same request to Musk during a G.O.P. meeting today . The White House didn\xe2\x80\x99t respond when I asked if they planned to do so.

To the extent other Republicans share Paul\xe2\x80\x99s concerns, most aren\xe2\x80\x99t willing to do anything about them just yet. \xe2\x80\x9cMost of us are aware that we gave up a lot of our authority,\xe2\x80\x9d Senator Mike Rounds told me, \xe2\x80\x9cbut I think that'll be revisited in the future, as other items of a unitary presidency are discussed\xe2\x80\x9d\xe2\x80\x94for instance, if the courts thoroughly stymie the president. But the reality, according to Paul, is: \xe2\x80\x9cThis is sort of the problem in Washington. [Republicans are] often for something when it\xe2\x80\x99s a Democrat president, [and] they\xe2\x80\x99re against it when it\xe2\x80\x99s a Republican president.\xe2\x80\x9d

\xe2\x80\x9cOur Founding Fathers Would Be Horrified\xe2\x80\x9d

Legally, Trump is justifying some of these latest tariffs under a 1962 and a follow-up 1977 law that gives the president the authority to impose import taxes during a \xe2\x80\x9cnational emergency.\xe2\x80\x9d (Paul also has legislation to sunset presidentially declared emergencies, and require a simple majority in Congress to vote to extend them; currently, there are nearly 40 such \xe2\x80\x9cnational emergencies\xe2\x80\x9d ongoing.) But Trump\xe2\x80\x99s policy justifications are manifold: fentanyl! Fairness! Jobs! Take your pick.

So Paul has introduced legislation, the No Taxation Without Representation Act, that would require Congress to approve any tariff\xe2\x80\x94even the ones already in place and justified by presidents under laws dating back to the Tariff Act of 1930. \xe2\x80\x9cI don\xe2\x80\x99t know how we came to the point that Congress said, \xe2\x80\x98Oh, well, we don\xe2\x80\x99t want anything to do with it,\xe2\x80\x99\xe2\x80\x9d Paul said. \xe2\x80\x9cYou\xe2\x80\x99re not even going to rubber stamp or even vote on them at all. The president could just put these taxes on trade anytime he wants. I think our Founding Fathers would be horrified.\xe2\x80\x9d But he says he hasn\xe2\x80\x99t spoken to anyone in the administration about the tariff policy because he doesn\xe2\x80\x99t think they\xe2\x80\x99re interested in hearing his viewpoint. \xe2\x80\x9cThey might be in three months or six months,\xe2\x80\x9d he said, \xe2\x80\x9cif our economy is teetering.\xe2\x80\x9d

Meanwhile, Paul has never managed to build much of a coalition in his own branch of government. Republicans tell me they\xe2\x80\x99re aware of his positions, but that he spends little time doing what most lawmakers do: making friends and persuading the skeptical. He\xe2\x80\x99s president of the Senate\xe2\x80\x99s theoretical party of \xe2\x80\x9cno\xe2\x80\x9d\xe2\x80\x94the small group of Republicans who reject most legislation and nearly all government funding\xe2\x80\x94that further isolates him when he pushes back against Trump. In fact, he\xe2\x80\x99s never had a piece of his own legislation signed into law, according to the Center for Effective Lawmaking\xe2\x80\x94and he has little influence in the party. In typical Paul fashion, his tariff bill from last Congress has no co-sponsors. But he\xe2\x80\x99s doing what he does best: causing trouble for the administration and leadership, and at a time when few have the gall or interest to do so.

Join Emmy Award-winning journalist Peter Hamby, along with the team of expert journalists at Puck, as they let you in on the conversations insiders are having across the four corners of power in America: Wall Street, Washington, Silicon Valley, and Hollywood. Presented in partnership with Audacy, new episodes publish daily, Monday through Friday.

Unique and privileged insight into the private conversations taking place inside boardrooms and corner offices up and down Wall Street, relayed by best-selling author, journalist, and former M&A senior banker William D. Cohan.
